docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Metabolic Flux Analysis Ontology (FLUXO) is an ontology that provides a structured framework for representing concepts, relationships, and data related to metabolic flux analysis. It covers experimental design, isotopic labeling, analytical instrumentation, computational methods, and statistical analysis used in isotope-based metabolic flux analysis (I-MFA) and related fluxomics workflows.

More information can be found at http://obofoundry.org/ontology/fluxo

docs.title: Stable release versions
docs.text:
The latest version of the ontology can always be found at:

http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/fluxo.owl

docs.title: Editors' version
docs.text: Editors of this ontology should use the edit version, src/ontology/fluxo-edit.owl

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• Browse FLUXO terms and their definitions in the OWL edit file
  • Cross-ontology mappings are available in SSSOM format
  • Use the ODK build pipeline to generate release artefacts (OWL, OBO, JSON): sh run.sh make prepare_release

docs.title: External Ontologies
docs.text:
FLUXO reuses terms and aligns with the following external ontologies and vocabularies:

Ontology Prefix Description IAO IAO Information Artifact Ontology COB COB Core Ontology for Biology and Biomedicine BFO BFO Basic Formal Ontology MSIO MSIO Metabolomics Standards Initiative Ontology CHEBI CHEBI Chemical Entities of Biological Interest PATO PATO Phenotype And Trait Ontology OBI OBI Ontology for Biomedical Investigations STATO STATO Statistics Ontology CHMO CHMO Chemical Methods Ontology NCIT NCIT National Cancer Institute Thesaurus UO UO Units of Measurement Ontology EDAM EDAM Ontology of data analysis methods SBO SBO Systems Biology Ontology nmrCV NMR nmrCV controlled vocabulary FOAF foaf Friend of a Friend vocabulary UniProt uniprot UniProt knowledgebase

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Please use this GitHub repository's Issue tracker to request new terms/classes or report errors or specific concerns related to the ontology.

docs.title: Acknowledgements
docs.text: This ontology repository was created using the Ontology Development Kit (ODK).

docs.title: Our Pledge
docs.text: In the interest of fostering an open and welcoming environment, we as contributors and maintainers pledge to make participation in our project and our community a harassment-free experience for everyone, regardless of age, body size, disability, ethnicity, gender identity and expression, level of experience, nationality, personal appearance, race, religion, or sexual identity and orientation.

docs.title: Our Standards
docs.text:
Examples of behavior that contributes to creating a positive environment include:

  • Using welcoming and inclusive language
  • Being respectful of differing viewpoints and experiences
  • Gracefully accepting constructive criticism
  • Focusing on what is best for the community
  • Showing empathy towards other community members

Examples of unacceptable behavior by participants include:

  • The use of sexualized language or imagery and unwelcome sexual attention or advances
  • Trolling, insulting/derogatory comments, and personal or political attacks
  • Public or private harassment
  • Publishing others' private information, such as a physical or electronic address, without explicit permission
  • Other conduct which could reasonably be considered inappropriate in a professional setting

docs.title: Our Responsibilities
docs.text:
Project maintainers are responsible for clarifying the standards of acceptable behavior and are expected to take appropriate and fair corrective action in response to any instances of unacceptable behavior.

Project maintainers have the right and responsibility to remove, edit, or reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, and other contributions that are not aligned to this Code of Conduct, or to ban temporarily or permanently any contributor for other behaviors that they deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, or harmful.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: This Code of Conduct applies both within project spaces and in public spaces when an individual is representing the project or its community. Examples of representing a project or community include using an official project e-mail address, posting via an official social media account, or acting as an appointed representative at an online or offline event. Representation of a project may be further defined and clarified by project maintainers.

docs.title: Enforcement
docs.text:
Instances of abusive, harassing, or otherwise unacceptable behavior may be reported by contacting the project team. All complaints will be reviewed and investigated and will result in a response that is deemed necessary and appropriate to the circumstances. The project team is obligated to maintain confidentiality with regard to the reporter of an incident. Further details of specific enforcement policies may be posted separately.

Project maintainers who do not follow or enforce the Code of Conduct in good faith may face temporary or permanent repercussions as determined by other members of the project's leadership.

docs.title: Attribution
docs.text: This code of conduct has been derived from the excellent code of conduct of the ATOM project which in turn is adapted from the Contributor Covenant, version 1.4, available at https://contributor-covenant.org/version/1/4

docs.title: Contributing to Metabolic Flux Analysis Ontology
docs.text:
:+1: First of all: Thank you for taking the time to contribute!

The following is a set of guidelines for contributing to FLUXO. These guidelines are not strict rules. Use your best judgment, and feel free to propose changes to this document in a pull request.

docs.title: Table Of Contents
docs.text:
• Contributing to Metabolic Flux Analysis Ontology
  • Table Of Contents
  • Code of Conduct
  • Guidelines for Contributions and Requests
    • Reporting problems with the ontology
    • Requesting new terms
    • Who can request a term?
    • How to write a new term request
    • How to add a new term
  • Best Practices
    • How to write great issues?
    • How to create a great pull/merge request?

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: The Metabolic Flux Analysis Ontology team strives to create a welcoming environment for editors, users and other contributors. Please carefully read our Code of Conduct.

docs.title: Reporting problems with the ontology
docs.text: Please use our Issue Tracker for reporting problems with the ontology. To learn how to write a good issue see here.

docs.title: Requesting new terms
docs.text:
Before you write a new request, please consider the following:

  • Does the term already exist? Before submitting suggestions for new ontology terms, check whether the term exist, either as a primary term or a synonym term. You can search for your term using OLS.
  • Can you provide a definition for the term? It should be very clear what the term means, and you should be able to provide a concise definition, ideally with a scientific reference.
  • Is the ontology in scope for the term? Sometimes, it is hard to tell whether a term "belongs" in and ontology. A rule of thumb is "if a similar term already exists, the new term is probably in scope." It can be very helpful to mention a very similar concept as part of the term request!

docs.title: Who can request a term?
docs.text: Anyone can request new terms. However, there is not guarantee that your term will be added automatically. Since this is a community resource, it is often necessary to do at least some of the work of adding the term yourself, see below.

docs.title: How to write a new term request
docs.text:
Request a new term via the GitHub Issue Tracker.

It is important to remember that it takes a lot of time for curators to process issues submitted to the tracker. To make this work easier, please always use issue templates if they are available (https://github.com/dtu-qmcm/mfa_ontology/issues/new/choose).

For how to write a good term request, please read the best practices carefully.

docs.title: How to add a new term
docs.text:
If you have never editted this ontology before, first follow a general tutorial

Process:

  1. Clone the repository (In case you are not an offical team member, create a fork first)
  2. Create new branch in git, for example git checkout -b issue123
  3. Open src/ontology/fluxo-edit.owl in your favourite editor, i.e. Protege. Careful: double check you are editing the correct file. There are many ontology files in this repository, but only one editors file!
  4. Perform your edit and save your changes
  5. Commit changes to branch
  6. Push changes upstream
  7. Create pull request

docs.title: How to write great issues?
docs.text: Please refer to the OBO Academy term request guide.

docs.title: How to create a great pull/merge request?
docs.text: Please refer to the OBO Academy best practices
